title: <x-subst>
breadcrumbs: XHTML Elements for Static Server Pages
html:
<x-subst>

<x-subst>

The <x-subst> element is a placeholder for an HTML element that will be generated in the HTML for a static server page (USP). It determines the location, type, and attributes of the HTML element that will be substituted.

<x-subst type =" Type " name =" Name " {TypeAttributes}> Content </x-subst> 

Example: 
 
       <x-subst type="submit" name="BTN_SAVE.ENTITY_A.MODEL">
       <input name="BTN_SAVE.ENTITY_A.MODEL" type="submit" />
       </x-subst>

Arguments

  • type—the type of the substitution; allowed values for Type are:

    • WidgetType—substitute a USP widget of the indicated type for the field that is bound to the element by the name. For more information, see <x-subst type="WidgetType">.

    • function—substitute a supported Proc function, as specified by the name. The supported functions are $urd_formtitle, $putmess, and $text. For more information, see <x-subst type="function">.

    • inclFile—insert the contents of an HTML or JavaScript file in the generated HTML. For more information, see <x-subst type="inclFile">.

  • name—name of the object to be used in the substitution; valid values depend on the type.

  • TypeAttributes—optional properties that will cause additional HTML attributes to be added to the generated element. For more information, see <x-subst type="WidgetType"> and Customizing the XHTML Generated for USPs.

  • Content—HTML that does not contain the opening or closing <x-subst> tags.

Description

Most <x-subst> tags are automatically created in the HTML layout of a USP when you copy the component data structure to the layout. Others are provided in the header of the default USP layout to substitute the component title or provide browser-side syntax checking. You can further customize the generated HTML by adding your own <x-subst> tags.

The webgen statement uses the <x-subst> tag to determine where dynamic content must be substituted when HTML output is generated. This includes fields, labels, functions, additional files, and field syntax checking elements.

Related Links
  • Static Server Pages

    Static Server Page (USP) components generate a complete web page every time they are activated. This makes them useful for relatively static web pages or a thin-client architecture in which all processing is done on the server. They can also be used for RESTful applications or file upload functionality.

  • Layout of Static Server Pages

    Static server page layouts are XHTML-based and include Uniface-specific tags to define the component data structure, as well as standard XHTML for defining the layout and appearance. The structure of the layout is best viewed in the XHTML source.

  • Insert Uniface Objects in the Server Page Layout

    When creating the layout, elements in the layout must be bound to the component data structure as defined in the Component Editor. You can do this by copying the Uniface objects from the component structure and inserting them as HTML in the layout.

  • Customizing the XHTML Generated for USPs

    The webgen command generates the XHTML document that is displayed in the browser in response to request for a static server page. You can influence the XHTML that it generates by manually editing the server page layout.

  • webgen

    Generate an XHTML document from the layout definition and the component definition of a static server page.
